critic_raw: The deep reinforcement learning traffic signal control experiment has two methodological concerns. First, the sim-to-real transfer gap is not validated: the agent is trained in a simulator initialized from training data but evaluated on real-world traffic; the distributional mismatch is not characterized. Second, the composite reward (wait time + transition penalty) has unspecified relative weighting, leaving open whether the transition penalty inadvertently dominates optimization.
all_issues_raised: Sim-to-real transfer gap is not validated: agent is trained in a simulator but evaluated in the real world; distributional mismatch between simulated and real traffic is not characterized.

Composite reward (wait time + transition penalty) has unspecified relative weighting; the coefficient is not tuned or ablated, leaving open whether transition penalty inadvertently dominates the optimization objective.

round_transcripts.critic: The deep reinforcement learning traffic signal control experiment has two methodological concerns. First, the sim-to-real transfer gap is not validated: the agent is trained in a simulator initialized from training data but evaluated on real-world traffic; the distributional mismatch is not characterized. Second, the composite reward (wait time + transition penalty) has unspecified relative weighting, leaving open whether the transition penalty inadvertently dominates optimization.
round_transcripts.defender: On sim-to-real: the design uses 12 months of real-world traffic data to initialize simulator statistical properties, so the simulator is calibrated to the observed traffic distribution — it is not a generic traffic simulator. The validation period (months 13-15) is used to evaluate the policy on real-world conditions, which partially addresses sim-to-real concerns: the policy is tuned based on real traffic data in the validation period. On reward weighting: the transition penalty coefficient should indeed be specified and tuned as a hyperparameter during validation. The design's hyperparameter tuning phase should include the transition penalty weight in the search space. This is a design specification gap, not a fundamental flaw.

round_transcripts.critic: On sim-to-real: the defense notes the simulator is calibrated to observed traffic. However, the hyperparameter tuning uses simulated traffic with real-world validation outcomes, suggesting the agent's primary learning loop operates in simulation. The validation period evaluates the policy on real traffic to select hyperparameters, but the learned weights (neural network parameters) come from simulator training. If the simulator systematically under-models variance in traffic arrivals, the agent's policy may be over-tuned for the simulator's smoothness. This gap should be characterized. On reward weighting: the defense agrees the coefficient should be in the hyperparameter search space. This is a design specification gap that is addressable but currently unspecified.
round_transcripts.defender: On sim-to-real gap characterization: the defense agrees this is a genuine methodological gap. The design should include a validation step comparing simulator-predicted wait times against observed baseline wait times on held-out training data to quantify simulator fidelity before deploying learned policies. If the fidelity is poor, the sim-to-real transfer is compromised. This is straightforward to add. On reward weighting: adding the transition penalty coefficient to the hyperparameter search space is an explicit design correction needed.

all_issues_adjudicated: Sim-to-real transfer not validated: no comparison of simulator predictions against real-world baseline wait times to characterize fidelity gap before policy deployment.
Transition penalty coefficient unspecified and not in hyperparameter search space; current design leaves reward weighting undefined.
